content:
Hey folks, now that we're slowly upstreaming our changes, I'd like to hear opinions on renaming PointerWrapper.

When I first introduced it over two years ago, it was basically just wrapping a pointer that was returned by open, used in a bunch of file callbacks, then given back to release.

Now we rightly use it to transfer ownership to C of objects that have a pointer-sized representation. While C owns it, it can be borrowed (and different original objects have different borrowed representations, GATs held us here!), mutably borrowed, and eventually can be given back to Rust.

So I was thinking of something like COwnable -- prefixing something with C isn't unprecedented (see CStr). But I'm not completely sold on the name. Any suggestions? Comments?
RawPtrOwnable or PtrOwnable? I'd avoid having c in the name, since in theory PointerWrapper can also be used to transfer ownership to (mostly unsafe) Rust code, although you don't have to.

Thanks Boqun!

I'd like to avoid Ptr or Pointer in the name because this is not restricted to pointers. Anything that fits in up to a pointer size can implement this
KBox looks like also a good fit for it?

Vincenzo Palazzo (vincenzopalazzo) said:

KBox looks like also a good fit for it?

I think KBox would be a bit misleading because it might give people the impression that it's a kernel Box.

UsizeOwnable and SmallOwnable sound promising. @y86 are there precedents for referring to things that fit in a usize Small?
There is the smallvec crate, but it can store arbitrary amounts of data inline, similarly also smallstr. I think UsizeOwnable sounds a bit weird.

small in these two cases refers to the number of elements being small enough to fit on the stack, no?

I think the weirdness of UsizeOwnable has to do with mixing size and ownership.

How about FFIOwnable?
Are you also considering changing the method name of *Ownable/PointerWrapper, right now it seems to bind with pointers because from_pointer and into_pointer.

One step back, maybe Ownable is the source of weirdness? In fact, theusize or ptr doesn't own the object (I'd say the heap owns the object because if we drop the usize or ptr, the object still exists in the heap).

Instead, usize or ptr represents or indexes the object, so how about UsizeRepresentable or UsizeIndexable?

The trait has to do with ownership because Rust gives up ownership to some C code, which is responsible for eventually giving ownership back -- if this doesn't happen, we have a leak. The C code also owns in the sense that Rust doesn't have any more references to it, so the C code may, for example, get mutable references to the object as long as it knows there are no other references on the C side alone (as the Rust side doesn't).

So saying that the object is representable with a usize is correct, but I feel is not a sufficient description of the trait.

The reason I liked FFIOwnable was that the it conveys the idea of ownership plus that the object needs to representable by something that is understood/compatible with FFI (implicitly a usize at the moment, things may change if/when we adopt CHERI)

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

The trait has to do with ownership because Rust gives up ownership to some C code, which is responsible for eventually giving ownership back -- if this doesn't happen, we have a leak. The C code also owns in the sense that Rust doesn't have any more references to it, so the C code may, for example, get mutable references to the object as long as it knows there are no other references on the C side alone (as the Rust side doesn't).

To me, the usage of PointerWrapper is to pass a Rust object through some C code and get back to Rust side, where C will NOT access the object (simply treat it as some sort of data). In this case, it's unclear to me that whether C owns the object ;-)

If we consider the cases where C can access the object (even free it), then it makes sense to say C owns the object. But I haven't seen a use case yet, so I hesitate to accept the trait should cover that. Maybe we can start with *Indexable and later on if we do have the use cases, we can always introduce a subtrait *Ownable. I'm always bad at prediction though, so I might be wrong ;-)

BTW, FFIOwnable or ForeignOwnable?

I see your argument but I'm not sure I agree that one needs to access or free the object to be considered the "owner". Though I'm open to being convinced otherwise :)

Let's look at the concrete struct file example: the open method returns a Result<T> where T: PointerWrapper. While the file is referenced, other methods on the trait (e.g., read/write/seek) are called with a <T as PointerWrapper>::Borrowed type (because they can be called concurrently, so this is a shared reference); eventually when the C side drops the file refcount to zero, it calls the release method, which takes a T as argument. The C side of course just stores an opaque value in file::private_data, but it manages the lifetime of that instance of T (handing ownership back to Rust when file is about to be freed). Doesn't it make sense to say that C owns the object despite not accessing it directly?
BTW, I do like ForeignOwnable, FFIOwnable has the unpleasant three uppercase letter at the beginning

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

I see your argument but I'm not sure I agree that one needs to access or free the object to be considered the "owner". Though I'm open to being convinced otherwise :)

Let's look at the concrete struct file example: the open method returns a Result<T> where T: PointerWrapper. While the file is referenced, other methods on the trait (e.g., read/write/seek) are called with a <T as PointerWrapper>::Borrowed type (because they can be called concurrently, so this is a shared reference); eventually when the C side drops the file refcount to zero, it calls the release method, which takes a T as argument. The C side of course just stores an opaque value in file::private_data, but it manages the lifetime of that instance of T (handing ownership back to Rust when file is about to be freed). Doesn't it make sense to say that C owns the object despite not accessing it directly?

Good point and good example.
So if C code has a call path to drop the object, it's fair to say the object is "ownable" by C

Thanks Boqun!

Anyone strongly opposes ForeignOwnable?

(I'll wait a couple of days and go with it if I don't hear anything)
